examples.title: DFA Simulator for Pattern Matching
examples.description: Simulates a DFA to check if input string contains 'ab' as substring
examples.code:
// DFA for strings containing 'ab'
// States: q0 = start, q1 = 'a' seen, q2 = 'ab' found

FUNCTION simulateDFA(input):
 currentState = q0
 FOR each character ch in input:
 IF currentState == q0 THEN:
 IF ch == 'a' THEN currentState = q1
 ELSE currentState = q0
 ELSE IF currentState == q1 THEN:
 IF ch == 'b' THEN currentState = q2
 ELSE IF ch == 'a' THEN currentState = q1
 ELSE currentState = q0
 ELSE: // q2
 currentState = q2 // stay in accept state
 RETURN (currentState == q2)

// Test
PRINT simulateDFA("ab") // TRUE
PRINT simulateDFA("aab") // TRUE
PRINT simulateDFA("bab") // FALSE
examples.explanation: The DFA tracks whether we've seen 'a' but not yet 'ab'. When 'ab' is found, it moves to accepting state q2 and stays there. This demonstrates how finite memory (just last symbol) can detect patterns.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = length of input

examples.title: Regular Expression to NFA Converter (Thompson's Construction)
examples.description: Simplified implementation showing NFA construction from regex
examples.explanation: Thompson's construction builds complex NFAs from simple ones using union, concatenation, and Kleene star operations. Each basic RE becomes an NFA, then they're combined. This is fundamental to regex engines.
examples.timeComplexity: O(m) where m = length of regex

examples.title: NFA to DFA Conversion (Subset Construction)
examples.description: Algorithm to convert NFA to equivalent DFA using ε-closure
examples.code:
// Compute ε-closure for a set of NFA states
FUNCTION epsilonClosure(S, nfa):
 stack = S
 closure = S
 WHILE stack is not empty:
 state = pop(stack)
 FOR each state t in nfa.transitions[state, 'ε']:
 IF t not in closure:
 closure.add(t)
 push(t, stack)
 RETURN closure

// Convert NFA to DFA
FUNCTION nfaToDfa(nfa):
 dfaStates = [] // list of state sets
 dfaTransitions = {}
 startSet = epsilonClosure({nfa.start}, nfa)
 dfaStates.append(startSet)
 marked = {} // track processed states
 
 WHILE exists unmarked state T in dfaStates:
 mark T as processed
 FOR each input symbol a in alphabet:
 U = {}
 FOR each state s in T:
 IF (s, a) in nfa.transitions:
 U = U ∪ nfa.transitions[s, a]
 U = epsilonClosure(U, nfa)
 
 IF U is not empty AND U not in dfaStates:
 dfaStates.append(U)
 dfaTransitions[T, a] = U
 
 // DFA final states are those containing NFA final state
 dfaFinal = {T for T in dfaStates if any(s in nfa.accept for s in T)}
 RETURN DFA(dfaStates, alphabet, dfaTransitions, startSet, dfaFinal)
examples.explanation: Subset construction converts NFA to DFA by treating each DFA state as a set of NFA states. The ε-closure computes all states reachable via ε-transitions. This proves NFAs and DFAs are equivalent.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n × m) where n = NFA states, m = alphabet size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(2^n) for storing power set of states

examples.title: Language Operations Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of basic language operations in Python
examples.explanation: These operations demonstrate how languages can be manipulated algebraically. Kleene star uses iterative concatenation until no new strings are generated - this is essentially the closure property of regular languages.
examples.timeComplexity: Varies by operation and language size
